items.hint: Short. Who you are, scope, what you are looking for, availability. No pleading.
items.model: Good morning. I am a house manager currently in my fourth year with a single family in Connecticut, running a principal residence of eleven thousand square feet with four staff reporting to me, plus a seasonal property. Before that, three years as a private PA in a London household. I am beginning to look at estate manager roles for a family with multiple residences, ideally on the East Coast, and I am not in a hurry. My CV is attached and references are available through both family offices. If it would be useful to have a conversation at any point, I would welcome it.
items.note: Scope in numbers, tenure in years, and no names anywhere. I am not in a hurry is the line that changes the register entirely, because unhurried candidates are the ones consultants take seriously. References through the office signals you understand how this works. It asks for a conversation rather than for consideration.

items.stem: A consultant asks why you left a role after fourteen months. Write your answer.
items.hint: Three or four sentences. Honest, brief, no blame, and it closes.
items.model: The family relocated permanently to Singapore and the role went with them. I was offered it and I thought seriously about it, but my mother had become unwell and I was not willing to be that far away. We parted on good terms and the family office will confirm all of that. It is the shortest tenure on my record and I would rather explain it early than have you wonder.
items.note: A real reason, stated once, with a verifiable source. Nobody is criticised. I would rather explain it early than have you wonder is the sentence that converts a weakness into evidence of judgement. The failure mode is a long account of what went wrong, which makes fourteen months sound like the least of it.

items.stem: Two weeks after a good call with a consultant, write your single follow up.
items.hint: Under sixty words. It carries information and asks for nothing.
items.model: Following up briefly. My situation has firmed up: I can now be available from the first of September rather than October, and I am open to a role with a travel component, which I was uncertain about when we spoke. Nothing needed from you, I just did not want you working from old information.
items.note: Every line is new information, and the change is a genuine one rather than manufactured. Nothing needed from you removes the obligation to reply, which paradoxically makes a reply more likely. It keeps you present in their mind without a single word about your hopes.

items.stem: A friend has told you about a role in a household they know. Write what you send your friend.
items.hint: Ask permission, do not ask for the introduction. Make it easy to say no.
items.model: Thank you for thinking of me, that was kind. Before I do anything, would you be comfortable with me mentioning your name if I approach whoever is running the search, or would you rather I did not. Either answer is completely fine and I would rather ask than assume. And if you would prefer to stay out of it altogether, just say so, it will not change anything between us.
items.note: It asks permission rather than requesting an introduction, which is a much smaller thing to grant. Either answer is completely fine is what makes the no genuinely available, and that is what protects the friendship. The person who asks this way is the person who gets recommended next time.

7.title: The interview
7.sub: The order a candidacy is actually built and run.

items.stem: Order the assembly of your candidacy.
steps: Write down the scope of every role you have held, in numbers
Build the CV around tenure and scope rather than duties
Write the covering letter to the specific household, not in general
Prepare your references and confirm each person is willing
Rehearse the three answers you know you will be asked
items.why: Numbers first, because scope is what you are actually selling and most people have never counted it. The CV is built from that material rather than from a list of tasks. The letter is written to one reader. References are confirmed before they are offered, since an unwarned referee is a risk. And rehearsal is last, because you cannot rehearse a story you have not yet assembled.

items.stem: Order how you handle an interview at the residence.
steps: Arrive early and wait out of sight until a few minutes before
Greet whoever opens the door with exactly the same courtesy as the principal
Let them lead the conversation before you present anything
Ask your questions about the household, not about the terms
Leave without asking what happens next, and write the same day
items.why: Early and unseen, because arriving flustered or arriving too visibly early both cost you. How you treat the person who opens the door is frequently reported back and is sometimes the whole test. You follow their structure. Your questions reveal what you care about. And leaving without pressing, then writing promptly, is the calm that this market reads as competence.

items.stem: You have been invited for a paid trial in the household. Order your conduct.
steps: Confirm in advance what you should wear and what time to arrive
Ask at the start what would make the day useful for them
Work to the household's existing methods, not your own
Write down what you observe and propose nothing
Thank the staff by name, not only the principal
items.why: Confirming beforehand removes the first two ways to start badly. Asking what would be useful reframes the day as service rather than performance. You use their methods, because a trial is not the moment to demonstrate improvements. You observe silently. And thanking the staff by name is the detail that gets reported upstairs, favourably, more often than any answer you gave.

items.stem: An offer has been made verbally. Order your response.
steps: Thank them warmly and express clear interest
Ask for the terms in writing before responding substantively
Read everything, including the confidentiality and notice provisions
Ask your questions in one batch rather than several
Accept in writing, and only then resign from your current role
items.why: Warmth first, so enthusiasm is never in question. Then paper, because a verbal offer is a hope. You read the clauses that govern the years, not the number that governs the month. Questions in one batch. And resignation follows written acceptance, never precedes it, which is the mistake that leaves people with nothing.

items.stem: A prospective household wants to speak to a former employer. Order your handling.
steps: Ask the household exactly what they want to verify
Contact your referee before giving out their details
Tell your referee what the role is and what will be asked
Provide the contact through the family office where one exists
Thank your referee afterwards regardless of the outcome
items.why: Knowing what is being verified lets you route it to the right person. Nobody's details are given out without warning. A briefed referee gives a better and more accurate reference. The office channel protects the principal's privacy. And the thank you is what keeps that reference available to you for the next ten years.

7.title: What are they measuring
7.sub: What is the question actually testing?

items.t: Tell me about your last principal
items.fb: Not a request for information. They are watching how much you are willing to say about someone who trusted you.

items.t: What would you do if you found something that worried you
items.fb: They want to see your escalation instinct, and whether you distinguish an emergency from an irritation.

items.t: Why did you leave after fourteen months
items.fb: Turnover is the problem they are paying to solve. They need the short tenure to have a reason.

items.t: We eat together in the kitchen here, how do you feel about that
items.fb: A test of fit with how this particular house lives, and there is no universally correct answer.

items.t: Have you ever had to let someone go
items.fb: Whether you can make an unpopular decision and describe it without either relish or evasion.

items.t: What is the most difficult situation you have handled
items.fb: Almost everyone answers this by disclosing a household's private crisis. The answer is in how you tell it, not what happened.

items.t: Where do you see yourself in five years
items.fb: They are asking whether you intend to still be here. Ambition described as movement reads badly.

items.t: The family are quite informal, is that a problem for you
items.fb: Fit. They are describing their house and asking whether you can live in it.

items.t: A guest asks you where the family will be next week, what do you say
items.fb: Movement class information. A direct test with a single correct instinct.

items.t: How would you spend your first month here
items.fb: Whether you observe before changing. Candidates who describe reorganising in week one do not get called back.

items.t: You have moved four times in six years, tell me about that
items.fb: The pattern is the question, not any individual move.

items.t: We have three dogs and a very busy house, how does that sound
items.fb: An honest description, and an invitation to be honest back. Enthusiasm you cannot sustain is not kindness.

7.title: In the room
7.sub: Mark the lines that are mistakes.

items.stem: A first meeting with the principal. Mark the mistakes.

lines.t: Greets the housekeeper who opens the door with full courtesy
lines.fb: Frequently the actual test, and it is always reported back.

lines.t: Comments admiringly on a painting in the hall
lines.fb: Unasked commentary on their possessions places you as a guest. Notice everything, remark on nothing.

lines.t: Answers a question about a past role without naming the family
lines.fb: They are watching for exactly this, and they will notice that you did it.

lines.t: Describes a previous principal's difficult behaviour to illustrate resilience
lines.fb: You have answered the real question, which was whether you would talk about them.

lines.t: Asks how the household prefers to communicate day to day
lines.fb: An operational question that shows you are thinking about the work.

lines.t: Asks about salary before fit has been discussed
lines.fb: A fair question, badly placed. It arrives once they want you.

items.stem: A paid trial in the household. Mark the mistakes.

lines.t: Asks at the start what would make the day most useful to them
lines.fb: It reframes the day as service rather than as an audition.

lines.t: Reorganises the linen cupboard to demonstrate initiative
lines.fb: Changing their systems on day one is read as judgement, and it is remembered.

lines.t: Uses the household's existing method even though a better one exists
lines.fb: Correct for a trial. The better method is proposed in month two, if at all.

lines.t: Photographs the pantry to remember the layout
lines.fb: You are not yet employed, and you would not do it if you were.

lines.t: Thanks each member of staff by name at the end of the day
lines.fb: This gets reported upstairs more often, and more favourably, than anything you said.

lines.t: Mentions to the chef what the last house paid for produce
lines.fb: Financial information about another household, disclosed casually. It is a breach, and it is heard as one.

items.stem: Terms are being discussed. Mark the mistakes.

lines.t: Asks for the terms in writing before responding in detail
lines.fb: Entirely normal, and it signals that you have done this properly before.

lines.t: Accepts immediately to demonstrate enthusiasm
lines.fb: Enthusiasm is shown in the warmth, not the speed. Instant acceptance also means the clauses went unread.

lines.t: Reads the confidentiality and notice provisions carefully
lines.fb: These govern years of your life. The number governs a month.

lines.t: Resigns the same afternoon on the strength of the conversation
lines.fb: Verbal offers evaporate. You resign after written acceptance, not before.

lines.t: Raises all remaining questions in one message
lines.fb: One batch. A drip of questions across three days reads as anxious.

lines.t: Asks to renegotiate a term already agreed in the previous meeting
lines.fb: Reopening a settled point damages trust more than the term is worth.

items.stem: Handling a reference request. Mark the mistakes.

lines.t: Warns the referee before giving out their details
lines.fb: An ambushed referee gives a worse reference, however fond of you they are.

lines.t: Gives the principal's mobile number directly
lines.fb: Where an office exists, the office is the channel. You do not hand out a principal's personal contact.

lines.t: Tells the referee what the role is and what will likely be asked
lines.fb: A briefed referee is accurate rather than coached.

lines.t: Asks the referee to emphasise a strength and omit a difficulty
lines.fb: You have asked someone to mislead on your behalf, and you have shown them who you are.

lines.t: Thanks the referee afterwards regardless of the outcome
lines.fb: It is why that reference is still available to you in ten years.

lines.t: Lists a referee they have not spoken to in four years without checking
lines.fb: People move, memories fade, and an out of date referee can sink a candidacy without meaning to.

7.title: Your candidacy
7.sub: Write it, then read the model.

items.stem: An interviewer asks: tell me about the most difficult situation you handled in your last household. Write your answer.
items.hint: Spoken. Demonstrate judgement while disclosing nothing about the family.
items.model: I will describe it without the details, if you do not mind, because it involved the family directly. There was a situation that developed quickly, out of hours, that had legal implications and could have reached the press. What I did was contain it physically first, then inform the family office within the hour in writing rather than by telephone, so there was a record. I did not tell any of the other staff anything at all, including the ones who could see something was happening. It resolved, and it never became public. The part I would do differently is that I should have had a written press protocol in place beforehand, and I wrote one afterwards.
items.note: The refusal to give details is stated at the start and framed as protecting them, which is itself the answer to the real question. The method is fully visible without the story being told. Ending on what you would do differently, with a concrete artefact you produced, converts a discretion answer into a judgement answer.

items.stem: Write the covering letter for an estate manager role with a family of four residences.
items.hint: Under two hundred words. Specific to them. Scope, tenure, one reason you fit.
items.model: Dear Ms Hartley, I am writing about the estate manager position you are handling for a family with residences on the East Coast and in Europe. I have spent the last four years as house manager to a single family in Connecticut, running an eleven thousand square foot principal residence with four staff reporting to me and a seasonal property in Maine, including its closing and opening each year. Before that I was a private PA in a London household for three years. The part of this role I am drawn to is the multiple property element. Running a house that stands empty for months taught me that the discipline is preventive rather than responsive, and I built the maintenance and closing protocols that the Maine property still runs on. I am available from September and able to travel without restriction. References are available through both family offices. I would welcome a conversation. With kind regards,
items.note: It names the specific element of the role and then proves competence in exactly that element with something built rather than something felt. Scope is in numbers, tenure in years, and no household is named. The whole letter is one page and answers the only question a consultant has: why this person for this role.

items.stem: You have an eight month gap in your record. Write how you address it on the CV and in person.
items.hint: One line for the CV, three or four sentences spoken. Factual, unapologetic.
items.model: On the CV: 2023, career break, family care, eight months. Spoken: My father was ill and I took eight months to look after him. It was a decision rather than a redundancy, and I would make it again. I kept in touch with the household throughout and they would have had me back if the timing had worked. It is on the CV rather than hidden in the dates because I would rather you heard it from me.
items.note: On paper it is one line, dated, and named, which stops it reading as concealment. Spoken, it is a decision rather than a misfortune, which changes how it lands entirely. The detail that the household would have had you back is the verifiable part that does the real work.

items.stem: Write the note you send the same day after an interview at the residence.
items.hint: Under eighty words. Warm, specific, and it asks for nothing.
items.model: Thank you for this morning, and please pass my thanks to Marta who looked after me before you came down. I came away with a clear picture of how the household runs, and the multiple property element is exactly the work I want to be doing. I am happy to answer anything further at any point. I will leave the timing entirely with you.
items.note: Thanking the person who opened the door is the line that gets read aloud. One specific thing you took from the conversation proves you were listening. And leaving the timing with them is the calm that this market consistently mistakes for seniority, correctly.

items.stem: Order your first week in a new household.
steps: Learn every name, role and length of service in the house
Find the water shut off, the electrical board and the alarm panel
Ask each person to show you how their work is currently done
Record everything and change nothing
Confirm your reporting line and thresholds in writing
items.why: Names first, because the staff decide your survival before the family does. The three physical locations are the ones that matter at two in the morning, and almost nobody asks in week one. You ask to be shown, which makes them the authority. You record and wait. And getting your thresholds in writing early is what protects you for the whole tenure.

items.stem: In month one you find a practice that is genuinely unsafe. Order your handling.
steps: Stop the unsafe practice immediately, without discussion
Explain the specific risk rather than criticising the person
Inform the family office the same day, in writing
Put the correct standard in writing and circulate it
Check it again in two weeks
items.why: Safety is the one thing that does not wait for your observation period. You attack the practice, not the person, or it will not stick. The office hears it from you first. Writing turns a correction into a standard that survives you. And the check in two weeks is what determines whether anything actually changed.

items.stem: Around week six you feel you have made a mistake taking the role. Order what you do.
steps: Recognise that this arrives for almost everyone at roughly this point
Change nothing structural and make no decisions for two weeks
Fix your sleep, your food and your days off first
Talk to someone outside the household, disclosing nothing about the family
Reassess only once you are rested
items.why: Knowing it is a stage rather than a verdict is most of the treatment. Decisions made in week six are almost always wrong and almost always regretted. The physical causes are addressed before the emotional interpretation, because exhaustion feels exactly like a wrong decision. You talk to someone, without breaching anything. And you judge the role only when rested.

items.stem: Order the close of your first ninety days.
steps: Review what you recorded in week one against what you now know
Write the household manual or update the one you inherited
Propose the changes you have been holding, all at once
Ask for a short review conversation with the office
Agree what the next ninety days should produce
items.why: Comparing your first impressions to your current knowledge is the single most useful hour of the whole period. The manual is the artefact that proves the ninety days. Proposals arrive together, as a considered plan rather than a stream of complaints. You ask for the review rather than waiting for one. And agreeing the next objective is what turns a probation into a career.

items.stem: Years later, you are leaving the household. Order your departure.
steps: Resign in writing, with proper notice, to the correct person
Say nothing to the other staff until the office has told them
Write or update the manual so your successor can function
Hand over in person, including the things that are not written down
Confirm afterwards that you remain bound by everything you signed
items.why: Proper channel, proper notice. The staff hear it from the household, not from you. The manual is your last professional act and it is the one that gets remembered. The unwritten handover is the generous part. And the closing confirmation matters because trust in this world has no end date, and saying so is what makes them refer you for the rest of your life.

items.t: Learning where the main water shut off is
items.fb: Day one or day two. It is the question that separates the professional from the newcomer.

items.t: Reorganising the household filing system
items.fb: Structural change waits until you understand why it is the way it is.

items.t: Stopping a practice that is genuinely unsafe
items.fb: Safety does not wait for your observation period. It is the one exception.

items.t: Telling the staff how the previous household did things
items.fb: It discloses another house and announces that you intend to change this one.

items.t: Asking each staff member to show you how they work
items.fb: It makes them the authority and you the student, which is the correct order.

items.t: Proposing a set of changes as a considered plan
items.fb: All at once, after ninety days, reads as a plan. Delivered piecemeal it reads as complaints.

items.t: Confirming your spending thresholds in writing
items.fb: Before you need them. Establishing this after a dispute is far harder.

items.t: Deciding to resign because week six feels unbearable
items.fb: The crash arrives for almost everyone at roughly that point. No decision is made in that state.

items.t: Writing or updating the household manual
items.fb: It is the artefact that proves the ninety days, and it cannot be written before you understand the house.

items.t: Fixing the one thing that is visibly and obviously failing
items.fb: One visible fix earns the right to propose more. Two or three at once does not.

items.t: Discussing your salary with another member of staff
items.fb: It damages you with everyone, including the people it appears to help.

items.t: Asking the office for a short review conversation
items.fb: You ask rather than wait. It is what turns a probation into an agreed objective.

8.title: Arriving and leaving
8.sub: Mark the lines that are mistakes.

items.stem: A new estate manager settling in. Mark the mistakes.

lines.t: Learns the names and tenure of every member of staff in the first days
lines.fb: The staff decide whether you survive long before the family does.

lines.t: Announces a new reporting structure in the first week
lines.fb: Structural change before understanding is read as judgement, and the house closes against you.

lines.t: Asks where the water shut off and the electrical board are
lines.fb: The questions that matter at two in the morning, asked before they are needed.

lines.t: Says the previous household did the linens differently and better
lines.fb: Two errors at once, disclosing another house and criticising this one.

lines.t: Fixes the one obviously broken thing and leaves everything else
lines.fb: One visible fix earns the right to propose more.

lines.t: Asks the housekeeper what the principal is really like
lines.fb: It invites gossip and it tells the house that you gather information about the family.

items.stem: The crash has arrived. Mark the mistakes.

lines.t: Recognises that this arrives for almost everyone at this point
lines.fb: Knowing it is a stage rather than a verdict is most of the treatment.

lines.t: Drafts a resignation letter that evening
lines.fb: Decisions made in week six are almost always wrong, and almost always regretted within a month.

lines.t: Takes the days off that were skipped in the first month
lines.fb: Exhaustion feels exactly like a wrong decision. Address the physical cause first.

lines.t: Confides in a colleague that the role may have been a mistake
lines.fb: It travels, it reaches the office, and it defines you before you have recovered.

lines.t: Speaks to someone outside the household without disclosing anything about the family
lines.fb: Entirely possible, and it is the pressure valve this profession requires.

lines.t: Reassesses the role once rested, two weeks later
lines.fb: The judgement is valid then. It was not valid before.

items.stem: Leaving after several years. Mark the mistakes.

lines.t: Resigns in writing to the correct person, with proper notice
lines.fb: Channel and notice. Both are remembered.

lines.t: Tells two close colleagues before the office announces it
lines.fb: The household announces departures. Pre announcing it destabilises the house and undermines the office.

lines.t: Updates the household manual so the successor can function
lines.fb: Your last professional act, and the one that is talked about afterwards.

lines.t: Takes a copy of the principal's book as a professional sample
lines.fb: It is the household's document containing the family's private information. Taking it is a breach that follows you.

lines.t: Hands over in person, including the unwritten knowledge
lines.fb: The generous part, and it costs you nothing you will miss.

lines.t: Tells the successor which staff members are difficult
lines.fb: You have prejudiced their working relationships before they began, and it will be repeated back.

items.stem: A year after leaving. Mark the mistakes.

lines.t: Continues to say nothing about the household, to anyone
lines.fb: Trust has no end date. Not the end of the contract, not the end of any agreement you signed.

lines.t: Uses the family's name in confidence to secure a new role
lines.fb: It ends the reference, and the consultant learns exactly what you would say about their client.

lines.t: Refers a good candidate to the household when they need someone
lines.fb: Still useful, still discreet. This is how people are hired for the rest of their careers.

lines.t: Posts a general reflection about working in private service
lines.fb: General and unattributable is fine, and it is how a professional reputation is built publicly.

lines.t: Answers a journalist's question because they no longer work there
lines.fb: Leaving changes nothing. This is the breach that ends a career permanently.

lines.t: Stays in touch with the family office once a year
lines.fb: Quiet, appropriate, and it keeps a reference alive for a decade.

8.title: Beginning and ending
8.sub: Write it, then read the model.

items.stem: Write the message you send the family office at the end of your first day.
items.hint: Under a hundred words. What you did, what you need, nothing about how it felt.
items.model: End of day one. I have met all five staff, noted names, roles and length of service, and asked each of them to walk me through their work this week. I have located the main water shut off, the electrical board and the alarm panel. Two things I need from you when convenient: my spending threshold and what sits above it, and confirmation of what comes to you rather than to the principal. Nothing is urgent. I am not proposing any changes for the first month.
items.note: It reports observable actions rather than impressions. The two requests are the two that protect you for the entire tenure, asked on day one when they are easy to ask. And the closing sentence tells the office exactly what they most want to hear from a new senior hire, which is that you are not about to start rearranging things.

items.stem: Write the opening of your ninety day review note, proposing your changes.
items.hint: Frame it as a plan. Observation, proposal, and what it costs. No complaints.
items.model: Ninety day review. Three proposals, all of which can wait if the timing is wrong. First, the maintenance schedule currently exists in three places and none of them agree. I would consolidate it into one calendar with named owners, which costs nothing and takes me a weekend. Second, four of our eleven suppliers have insurance certificates expiring within six months and there is no reminder system. I would add expiry tracking with a sixty day alert. Third, the guest wing standard is not written down, so it varies by whoever prepares it. I would document it, one page, with photographs. None of these change how the house runs. They make it survive whoever is running it.
items.note: Three, not nine, and each one is observation then proposal then cost. All of which can wait if the timing is wrong removes any pressure and makes agreement easy. The closing line is the argument that senior households respond to, because continuity is the thing they are actually buying.

items.stem: Write your resignation letter after four years in a household.
items.hint: Short. Notice, gratitude, handover offer. No reasons unless they are simple.
items.model: Dear Mr Aldridge, I am writing to give notice of my resignation, with my last day being the thirtieth of June, which gives the eight weeks my contract requires. I have been offered a role with a larger portfolio of properties and it is a step I want to take. It is not a decision about this household, which I have been glad to serve for four years. I will have the household manual fully updated before I go, and I would like to hand over to my successor in person if the timing allows. I remain bound by my confidentiality obligations, which I understand do not end with my employment, and I would want you to know that I regard them as permanent. Thank you for four good years.
items.note: Notice period stated by date and by contract, so nothing has to be calculated. One simple reason, and the explicit statement that it is not about them. The confidentiality paragraph is the one most people omit, and it is the paragraph that gets you referred for the next decade.

items.stem: Write the opening of the handover note you leave for your successor.
items.hint: Generous and practical. The things that are not in the manual.
items.model: Everything operational is in the manual, which is current to this week. This note is the part that is not written down anywhere. The principal reads messages first thing and rarely after eight in the evening, so anything needing a decision should land before seven. Thursdays are heavier than they look, the board pack arrives Wednesday evening and the study needs to be ready by six. Marta has been here eleven years and knows more about this house than the manual does, ask her before you change anything. The heating in the east wing takes four hours to respond, not one. And the family office prefers one written summary on Friday to messages across the week. Good luck. It is a good house.
items.note: It gives the successor rhythm, thresholds and people, which is exactly what a manual cannot hold. Naming Marta and directing them to her is generous and also protects the house. It is a good house is the last line, and leaving that behind rather than a warning is what people remember about you years later.
